titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Goldie Russler and Abraham Burstein papers
titlestmt.author: Eve C. Neiger

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

controlnote.p: This collection contains 2 published digital objects. And those objects are comprised of 14 digital files.

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Wednesday May 7, 2025 at 23:02

did.unittitle: Goldie Russler and Abraham Burstein papers

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: In English, Yiddish, Hebrew, and Russian.

did.abstract: The Goldie Russler and Abraham Burstein Papers consist of personal and professional records of American Yiddish theater actress Goldie Russler (1904-1997) and her husband Abraham Burstein (1900-1992) from circa 1900-1992.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.
Box 12 (audio tapes): Use of originals is restricted. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased jointly with the Judaica Collection from Lorne Bair Rare Books on the Sinclair Lewis Fund and the Rosalyn and Joseph Newman Collection Fund, 2016.
August 2023 acquisition: Gift of Stefan Weisman, 2023.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The Goldie Russler and Abraham Burstein Papers is arranged into five groupings: I. Goldie Russler, 1925-1988; II. Abraham Burstein, 1930-1991; III. Russler and Burstein family papers, 1930-1992; IV. Photographs, 1900-1980; V. August 2023 acquisition, 1920-1992.

bioghist.head: Goldie Russler (1904-1997)
bioghist.p: Goldie Russler was a Jewish actress active in American Yiddish theater in New York in the 1920s and 1930s, and in the Artef (Arbeter Teater Farband), an American Yiddish theater company. Goldie was born on August 1, 1904 to Hetya and Menachem Aaron Russler in Klishkovtsy, Bessarabia, Russian Empire (Klishkivtsi, Ukraine) and had five siblings, Yona (Weisman), Ephraim, Shoshana, and Benjamin, some of whom later moved to Israel. Goldie immigrated to the United States in 1920, arriving in New York on the Nieuw Amsterdam on September 7, 1920 under the name Golda Rosler. She performed in amateur theater productions throughout the 1920s. Goldie performed in her first Artef production, Aristocrats , in 1929 and went on to appear in fourteen Artef productions from 1929-1938 including Rekrutn (1934), Yegor Buliche (1933-1934), and Dostigayev (1934-1935). She was a member of Artef's 1938-1939 traveling troupe and directed productions at Camp Kinderland in Hopewell Junction, New Jersey. Goldie became a naturalized United States citizen on December 19, 1938. She married Abraham Burstein on June 14, 1946 and changed her name to Goldie Burstein. She lived in New York City until her death on December 5, 1997.

bioghist.head: Abraham Burstein (1900-1992)
bioghist.p: Abraham (Abram) Burstein was born on June 12, 1900 in Bessarabia, Russian Empire (Ukraine) to Rabbi Shmuel Burstein and Miriam Shaindel Cahane, and had five siblings Sheiva, Rabbi Meyer Cohen Burstein, Pinchas, Sarah, and Hanna. His mother and sister Hanna were murdered at Babi Yar in September 1941. Abraham immigrated to the United States on June 2, 1921. He founded the Manhattan Slipper Company in 1928 which he ran until the late-1950s. Abraham married Yiddish theater actress Goldie Russler on June 14, 1946. He later worked as an economist. Abraham lived in New York City until his death on April 30, 1992.

bioghist.head: Artef (Arbeiter Teater Farband) (circa 1929-1940)
bioghist.p: The Arbeter Teater Farband (Workers Theater Association), known by the acronym, Artef, was an American Yiddish theater group organized circa 1929 by the Young Workers' League. The Artef was influenced by communist groups and often performed works that reflected an American Jewish proletarian ideology. The Artef performed plays in both Yiddish and English gaining a wider audience in the late 1930s. At its founding Kalman Marmor was president, Y. Adler, G. Sander and David Abrams were secretaries and Jacob Mestel was the director. The Artef was dissolved in 1940.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: The Goldie Russler and Abraham Burstein Papers. Yale Collection of American Literature, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit. This collection received a basic level of processing, including rehousing and minimal organization. Information included in the Description of Papers note and Collection Contents section is drawn from information supplied with the collection and from an initial survey of the contents. Folder titles appearing in the contents list below are often based on those provided by the creator or previous custodian. Titles have not been verified against the contents of the folders in all cases. Otherwise, folder titles are supplied by staff during initial processing. This finding aid may be updated periodically to account for new acquisitions to the collection and/or revisions in arrangement and description. These materials have been arranged and described according to national and local standards. For more information, please refer to the Beinecke Manuscript Unit Processing Manual. Yiddish and Hebrew names, titles, and quotations in their original languages have been transliterated in accordance with Library of Congress guidelines. Some nitrate negatives were found among the photographs. Negatives matching extant prints were discarded in 2019 in compliance with Yale University policy. Negatives lacking matching prints in 2019 and 2023 were digitized and then discarded following digitization.

scopecontent.head: Content Description
scopecontent.p: The Goldie Russler and Abraham Burstein Papers consist of personal and professional records of American Yiddish theater actress Goldie Russler (1904-1997) and her husband Abraham Burstein (1900-1992) from circa 1900-1992. The papers include personal correspondence between the couple, correspondence to and from family members in the United States, Israel, and Russia, as well as some professional correspondence from Russler's association with the Artef (The Arbeter Teater Farband) and from Burstein's business enterprises. Russler's papers include scripts, manuscript music parts and song texts, photographs, clippings and other records related to her career as a Yiddish theater actress with Artef. Clippings are primarily in Yiddish and includes articles about Russler, Artef, and Yiddish theater from Morgen Freiheit and other Yiddish language periodicals. Burstein's papers include business and financial records, correspondence, records of membership in philanthropic organizations, records related to wills and burial plots, and material related to family. Also included are Russler and Burstein family photographs and correspondence, primarily in Yiddish, Hebrew, and Russian. Records related to family history, before and after World War II, are included among the correspondence.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Goldie Russler and Abraham Burstein Papers is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

did.unittitle: Baym telefon ( By the Telephone ) and A Jewish Tribunal by David Bergelson
Die Kaprizne Kalemoyd ( The Capricious Bride ) adapted from Abraham Goldfaden by Y. Dobrushin
The World to Come by Moishe Nadir
Manuscript sheet music and song lyrics
Theater programs from Israel and the United States
Clippings related to Goldie Russler, Artef, and Yiddish theater
Ten Years Artef (New York: Posy-Shoulson Press, 1937)
Material related to YKUF (Yiddish Kultur Farband)
Goldie Russler correspondence to Abraham Burstein
Papers relating to the education of Zvi Leibovitz
Correspondence and records related to the Manhattan Slipper Company
Correspondence and records related to the estate of Fannie Penn
Burstein family correspondence from Russia, Israel, and the United States
Clippings and other ephemera related to family
The Philistine: A periodical of protest

did.materialspec: High background noise is present. The program is distorted at times. Audio, at times, sounds hollow or unclear. Audio is unbalanced. The right channel decreases in volume at about 00:07:23. The program is in silence from 00:17:49-00:18:20 and 00:20:58-00:53:37. High amounts of hum are present. High amounts of buzz can be heard. The right channel drops out around 00:53:37.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Box 12: Restricted fragile material (audio cassette tape). Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

did.unittitle: Photographs of Goldie Russler and Abraham Burstein

descriptivenote.p: Photographs of Goldie Russler and Abraham Burstein

did.unittitle: Color photographs of Dr. Burstein's retirement luncheon
Black and white cellulose acetate negatives
Black and white cellulose acetate negatives

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Nitrate negatives matching extant prints were discarded in 2019 in compliance with Yale University policy. Nitrate negatives lacking matching prints were digitized and then discarded following digitization.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This accession contains playscripts, programs, and correspondence, primarily between Goldie Russler and Abraham Burstein.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Maintains order found at time of acquisition.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Four nitrate negatives were found and are being digitized. The files will be available for public access. The negatives will be discarded following digitization in compliance with Yale University policy.

did.unittitle: Programs, appointment cards, and other papers

did.materialspec: Digitized images of four nitrate negatives were created and are available for public access. The negatives were discarded following digitization in compliance with Yale University policy.
